title_en: Introduction to the Maliki School and the Commentary Tradition
title_ar: مقدمة في المذهب المالكي وتقليد الحاشية
content_en:
Hashiyat al-Dusuqi ala al-Sharh al-Kabir is one of the most authoritative reference works in the Maliki school of Islamic jurisprudence. Composed by Sheikh Muhammad ibn Ahmad al-Dusuqi (d. 1230 AH / 1815 CE), it takes the form of a supercommentary — a hashiyah — written upon al-Sharh al-Kabir of Imam Ahmad al-Dardir (d. 1201 AH / 1786 CE), which is itself a commentary on Mukhtasar Khalil, the foundational digest of Maliki fiqh by Khalil ibn Ishaq al-Jundi (d. 776 AH / 1374 CE). This three-tiered structure — matn, sharh, hashiyah — is typical of the mature Islamic legal tradition and reflects how each generation of scholars built upon and refined the understanding of its predecessors.

The Maliki school takes its name from Imam Malik ibn Anas (d. 179 AH / 795 CE) of Madinah, the city of the Prophet's migration. A hallmark of Maliki legal methodology is the use of the 'amal of Madinah (the lived practice of the people of Madinah) as a source of law alongside the Quran and the Sunnah. Imam Malik argued that the continuous practice of the Prophet's city carries evidentiary weight comparable to a well-attested hadith, since that practice represents the transmission of prophetic norms through action rather than oral report alone.

Another distinctive Maliki principle is maslaha mursalah — the recognition of public interest as a source of law in cases where no specific text addresses the question. Maliki jurists applied this principle extensively in governance, taxation, and social regulation, giving the school a reputation for pragmatic adaptability within the framework of Islamic principles. Al-Dusuqi's hashiyah reflects these methodological commitments throughout, frequently invoking both the practice of Madinah and considerations of public interest in resolving contested questions.

The hashiyah form allowed al-Dusuqi to engage critically with al-Dardir's sharh, noting errors, adding nuance, recording scholarly disagreements that al-Dardir had not mentioned, and updating the analysis with later Maliki scholarship. This critical engagement makes the Hashiyat al-Dusuqi essential reading not merely as a repository of Maliki rulings but as a demonstration of the living tradition of Islamic legal scholarship.
